scenario: A loan-approval process completes the main flow at one of three end events: "approved," "rejected," "pending-manual-review." None of them throws errors or signals; each just finishes the path normally.
question: Which End Event type fits?

options.text: Terminate End Event kills the entire process instance, including all active parallel tokens, when reached. Using it for each of three normal outcome branches would end the whole instance from any branch, which is not the intent here. Each branch should terminate its own token independently without forcing global termination. Teams building on Camunda 8 should consider this when designing process models for production deployment.

Error End Event throws a named BPMN error upward to a parent scope or boundary event handler. These three outcomes -- approved, rejected, and pending-manual-review -- are normal business results, not error conditions. Using Error End Events here would misrepresent normal outcomes as exceptional failures and mislead diagram readers. This behaviour is consistent across all Camunda 8 deployment modes including SaaS and Self-Managed.

None End Event represents normal completion of a flow path by consuming the arriving token. Multiple None End Events in different branches express distinct business outcomes clearly. Readers see at a glance how the process can end -- approved vs rejected vs pending-review -- without each implying error or global termination.

A single shared End Event with conditional sequence flows from a preceding gateway would collapse the three outcomes into one visual element, losing the readable distinction between approved, rejected, and pending outcomes. The model works but reduces clarity and hides the multiple business results from diagram readers. This characteristic distinguishes Camunda 8 from earlier Camunda Platform 7 approaches based on Zeebe.

a.text: Incorrect. None End Event terminates the current token normally. Multiple None End Events are good BPMN style for representing distinct outcomes (e.g., approved vs rejected) -- readers see at a glance how the process can end.

b.text: Incorrect. Terminate kills the whole instance; here each branch independently ends.

c.text: Correct. Error End throws an error upward; the outcomes are normal business results, not errors.

d.text: Incorrect. Single end + gateway works but loses the visual cue of "these are different outcomes."

explanation: Error End throws an error upward; the outcomes are normal business results, not errors.
